coredata.dc:title: Application of DNA aptamers as sensing layers for detection of carbofuran by electrogenerated chemiluminescence energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuhuai
Wu, Xuejin
Liu, Chunhua
Yin, Guihao
Luo, Jinhui
Xu, Zhi
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) sensing platform for carbofuran detection was constructed based on ECL energy transfer (ECRET) and carbon dot (C-dot)-tagged aptamers as the recognition element. Fullerene (C60)-loaded gold nanoparticles (C60-Au) were used as the energy donor, modified on a glassy carbon electrode. C-dot-tagged DNA aptamers were used as the receptor, and ECRET then occurred between C60-Au and C-dots. After accepting the energy, the C-dots acted as a signal indicator and showed decreased signal intensity in the presence of targets, which competitively bound to DNA aptamers and blocked energy transfer. Using this robust, straight-forward strategy, the sensor showed a linear ECL response to carbofuran at concentrations from 2.0 × 10−11 mol L−1 to 8.0 × 10−9 mol L−1. The detection limit of this assay was shown to be 8.8 × 10−13 mol L−1. Thus, the sensing approach described in this study could be adapted for use in the detection of various pesticide residue targets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptasensor
Fullerene
Electrogenerated chemiluminescence
Gold nanoparticles
Carbofuran

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the C60-loaded gold nanoparticle-modified electrode
Characterisation of the aptasensor using CV and AC
Investigation of the ECL energy transfer system
Optimising the response of the aptasensor to CBF

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support received from the Fundamental Scientific Research Funds for Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences (grant nos. 1630042015011 and 1630042015009 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Procedure for aptamer sensor fabrication for the detection of carbofuran (CBF).
(A) SEM of C60-Au; (B) SEM of Au in C60-Au composites; (C) UV-VIS of C60-Au composites; (D) XRD of C60-Au composites.
CVs (A) and ACs (B) of the aptasensor under different conditions: a. Bare GCE; b. C60-Au-modified GCE; c. C60-Au/DNA aptamer-modified GCE; d. C60-Au/DNA aptamer/CBF-modified GCE.

ECLs of the aptasensor under different conditions: a. ECL of C60-Au; b. ECL of C-dots; c. ECL of C-dots in the presence of C60; d. ECL of C-dots in the presence of Au; e. ECL of C-dots in the presence of C60-Au; f. ECL of C-dots of the aptasensor after adsorption of CBF.
Effect of pH (a) and adsorption time (b) on fluorescent intensity.
ECL of aptasensor with CBF adsorbed from different concentrations. a ∼ n: (0, 2, 5, 15, 30, 60, 120, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800) × 10−11 mol L−1 CBF.

The of ECL: aptasensor treatment with no CBF(1), 2 × 10−9 mol L−1 CBF (2), 2 × 10−9 mol L−1 CBF containing 1 × 10−7 mol L−1 of aldicarb (3), imidacloprid 4), abamectin (5), dimethomorph (6), acetamiprid (7), carbary (8), metolcarb (9), pirimicarb (10), isoprocarb (11), fenobucarb (12), tsumacide (13) and the mix of above mentioned pesticides (14).
Comparing different sensor methods of detecting CBF.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of sample assay and recoveries.

head.ce:title: Application of DNA aptamers as sensing layers for detection of carbofuran by electrogenerated chemiluminescence energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Haikou, 571101, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Tropical Products (Haikou) Ministry of Agriculture, Haikou, 571101, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Tropical Products (Haikou) Ministry of Agriculture

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Haikou, 571101, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Haikou, 571101, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, an electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) sensing platform for carbofuran detection was constructed based on ECL energy transfer (ECRET) and carbon dot (C-dot)-tagged aptamers as the recognition element. Fullerene (C60)-loaded gold nanoparticles (C60-Au) were used as the energy donor, modified on a glassy carbon electrode. C-dot-tagged DNA aptamers were used as the receptor, and ECRET then occurred between C60-Au and C-dots. After accepting the energy, the C-dots acted as a signal indicator and showed decreased signal intensity in the presence of targets, which competitively bound to DNA aptamers and blocked energy transfer. Using this robust, straight-forward strategy, the sensor showed a linear ECL response to carbofuran at concentrations from 2.0 × 10−11 mol L−1 to 8.0 × 10−9 mol L−1. The detection limit of this assay was shown to be 8.8 × 10−13 mol L−1. Thus, the sensing approach described in this study could be adapted for use in the detection of various pesticide residue targets.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbofuran (CBF) [1] is a broad-spectrum carbamate pesticide that is commonly used in agriculture worldwide. CBF alone is highly toxic, and animal studies have suggested that CBF found in fresh produce may cause health problems. Therefore, it is necessary for us to detect and monitor CBF residues in farm products and the environment. Currently, CBF is mainly detected by high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [2], fluorescence [3], and electrochemical methods [4]. However, the use of HPLC-MS is limited owing to the high cost of operation and complex pretreatment requirements. Additionally, the stability and reproducibility of fluorescence and electronic methods need to be further improved, and CBF residues are present in trace quantities in real samples, i.e., less than 1 × 10−9 mol L−1. Accordingly, it is necessary to develop a sensitive, selective, inexpensive, and simple method for the detection of CBF.

Aptamers [5,6], including DNA aptamers [7], RNA aptamers [8], and peptides [9], are oligonucleotide or peptide molecules that bind to specific target molecules. In recent years, aptamer-base sensors have attracted much attention in medical immunology [10], bioanalysis [11], and environmental analysis fields [12], due to the selectivity, affinity, versatile target binding, and relatively low cost of aptamers. Among these sensors, electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) sensors [13] have the unique advantages of high sensitivity, wide linear range, low detection limit, convenience, and controllability. When the active substance (called the electrochemical donor) transfers energy to ECL reagents (acceptors) under a potential, the signal intensity of ECL is greatly enhanced; this phenomenon is referred to as ECL energy transfer (ECRET) [14,15]. Thus, the sensitivity of ECRET-based sensors is much higher than that of normal ECL sensors. However, no studies have reported the use of ECRET-based aptamer sensors for the detection of residual pesticides.

Fullerene C60 (C60) [16] has attracted much attention owing to its unique electron transport behaviour, optical and physical properties, mechanical features, and potential applications, including use as sensors [17], for gas storage [18], and as sensory reinforced metals [19]. C60 can combine with other inorganic [20,21] or organic materials [22] to form a composite material that exhibits excellent performance, possibly due to the synergistic effects of the added materials. C60 and its composites may function as energy and electronic donors [23]. Carbon dots (C-dots) [24], a new type of carbon nanomaterial, possess unique ECL properties [25], low toxicity [26], and good biocompatibility [27], making them promising materials for applications in ECL. Moreover, as novel ECRET reagents, C-dots have many excellent photoelectric properties that traditional organic molecules do not possess, including the dielectric effect, size distribution effect, and nano-confined effect for electronic and photonic molecules [28,29]. However, the application of ECRET as an ECL sensor using the C60 nano-composite and C-dots has not been studied.

In this study, we fabricated an ECL sensor based on ECRET between C60-loaded gold nanoparticles (C60-Au) and C-dots, with aptamers as recognition elements for CBF detection. C60-Au was synthesised using a simple one-step hydrothermal method and modified on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). First, C-dots-tagged aptamer sequences were synthesized using systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) [30]. Then C-dots-tagged aptamers were linked to the C60-Au-modified GCE by the thiol in the aptamers. Under certain conditions, ECL energy transfer occurred between C60-Au and C-dots, in which C60-Au acted as the energy donor and C-dots acted as the acceptor. After accepting the energy, the ECL of C-dots was greatly enhanced. In the process of CBF testing, C-dots acted as a signal indicator, with decreased signal intensity observed in the presence of the target after competitive binding of the target to DNA aptamers, blocking energy transfer (Fig. 1 ). The mechanism for the inhibition may be related to the tertiary structure of DNA aptamers. Before CBF binding to DNA aptamers, C-dots were most likely closer to the transduction surface, explaining the higher ECL output. Upon binding to the CBF, this structure was likely extended, leaving the C-dot farther away from the electrode surface and resulting in lower signals. The decrease in ECL intensity was linear with the CBF concentration, establishing a new method for CBF detection. Due to the ECRET and C60-Au amplification, the detection signals were amplified effectively. Therefore, the aptamer sensor exhibited high linearity, stability, sensitivity, and resolution and may have applications for the detection of CBF.

The ECL measurements were carried out on a Model MPI-E ECL analyser (Xi'an Remex Instrument Co. Ltd., China) using a three-electrode system. Electroanalytical measurements, including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and alternating current (AC), were performed on a standard three-electrode workstation (CHI660E; Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China). The three-electrode system consisted of an Ag/AgCl electrode containing saturated KCl solution as the reference electrode, a platinum wire electrode as the auxiliary electrode, and a modified gold electrode (d = 2 mm) as the working electrode.

CBF aptamer sequences were synthesised by Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), with the following sequence: 5′-C-dots(CH2)3 CACCTGGGGGAGTATTGCGGAGGAAAGAGAACACTGG GGCAGATATGGGCCAGCAGGTC(CH2)3 SH-3′, and stored at −20 °C out of direct light. CBF, aldicarb, imidacloprid, abamectin, dimethomorph, acetamiprid, carbary, metolcarb, pirimicarb, isoprocarb, fenobucarb, and tsumacide were obtained from the Agricultural Environmental Quality Supervision and Testing Centre of China. 6-Mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. Additionally, 3 × 10−4 mol L−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution was prepared with 0.5 mol L−1 KCl, and the molar ratios of Fe(CN)6 3−∶Fe(CN)6 4− was 1:1. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ, Milli-Q, and Millipore).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the C60-loaded gold nanoparticle-modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The GCE electrode was dipped into acetone and ethanol and then cleaned ultrasonically for 5 min. Next, C60-loaded gold nanoparticles were self-assembled on the GCE functionalised with 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane as previously reported [31]. GCE was prepared by treatment with toluene solution containing unmodified C60 (1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1) and 4-aminothio-phenoxide/hexane-protected gold nanoparticles (1.5 × 10−4 mol L−1). Then, the amination reaction of C60 facilitated self-assembly of nanoparticles on the GCE.

The aptamer biosensor platform was constructed on a C60-Au-modified GCE, on which CBF aptamers were immobilised on the GCE through the Au-S interaction [32]. C60-Au-modified GCE was dipped in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) containing 1.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 CBF aptamer for aptamer immobilisation. After immersing the cleaned GCE for 1 h, the GCE surfaces were rinsed with ultrapure water and passivated with 1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 MCH in water for 4 h to obtain the aptasensor [33]. Before the ECL test, the aptasensor was immersed in PBS containing different concentrations of CBF for 10 min.

The electrochemical experiments were performed in 3.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.5 mol L−1 KCl. CV was performed over a potential range from −0.2 V to +0.6 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. AC was performed at a potential of 0.19 V over the frequency range of 100 mHz to 100 kHz using an alternating voltage of 5 mV. The ECL test was conducted in 10 mL of 0.05 mol L−1 Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.8). The ECL measurement was performed by CV from −0.3 V to +0.8 V with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. The voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set to 700 V. The ECL signal-time curve was created with continuous potential scanning for five cycles with a magnification of 3. ECL signals related to the CBF concentrations were recorded.

Vegetables samples (20.0 g) were treated with a high-speed homogeniser with 40 mL acetonitrile and transferred to a 100-mL beaker containing 5.0 g NaCl. After stirring and filtration, the filtrate was dried by rotary evaporation. The final residue was then dissolved in 5.0 mL methanol.

The microstructure and morphology of the prepared C60-Au were characterised by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), ultraviolet-visible (UV-VIS) absorption spectrum analysis, and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fig. 2 A and Fig. 2B show the morphology of C60-Au composites. C60-Au was regularly spherical, tended to form small clusters, and had a diameter of about 600 nm (Fig. 2A). Moreover, gold nanoparticles were clearly observed on the surface of C60, which had a uniformly distributed granular shape that was regularly spherical with a size of about 5 nm (Fig. 2B). The absorption spectra of C60-Au are shown in Fig. 2C. The C60-Au exhibited obvious absorption at 530 nm, which could be attributed to the gold nanoparticles. Absorption peaks were also observed at 275, 310, and 360 nm, which could be attributed to C60 [34]. The absorption spectrum of C60-Au exhibited unique features [35], including extension into the red-near infrared region [36]. This absorption behaviour was typical for clusters of C60. XRD was used to characterise the structure and composition of C60-Au. As shown in Fig. 2D, the three diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 10.80°, 17.60°, and 20.70° corresponded to the (111), (200), and (220) diffraction planes, respectively, of C60 (curve a) [37]. For C60-Au, in addition to the characteristic peaks of C60, we also observed four diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 38.20°, 44.40°, 64.50°, and 77.50°, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) diffraction planes of Au nanoparticles [38], respectively. These results demonstrated that the C60-Au composites were successfully synthesised.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of the aptasensor using CV and AC
ce:section.ce:para: CV and AC were used to characterise the changes on the GCE surface. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the peak current was lower for the C60-Au-modified GCE (curve b) relative to that for the unmodified GCE electrode (curve a) because the C60-Au hindered electron transport to K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6]. Additionally, as the DNA aptamers and CBF were adsorbed on the sensor, the peak current constantly decreased (curves c and d). Consistent with this, the resistances of GCE (curve a) were gradually increased after C60-Au (curve b), DNA aptamers (curve c), and CBF (curve d) were assembled onto GCE one by one, as shown in Fig. 3B. These results indicated that the aptasensor was incorporated onto the GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the ECL energy transfer system
ce:section.ce:para:
Next, we examined the ECRET between C60-Au and C-dots. The results are shown in Fig. 4 . There was no ECL signal for the C60-Au solution (curve a). However, there was an obvious ECL signal for the C-dot solution under a certain voltage (curve b). Further analysis showed that C60 could not enhance the ECL intensity of C-dots when C60 and C-dot-tagged DNA aptamers simultaneously modified on GCE because of C60 acted as an electron receptor (curve c) rather than an electron donor [39]. However, the ECL intensity of C-dots bound to the aptamers was obviously enhanced by Au nanoparticles (curve d) on GCE, which acted as an efficient electron donor. Moreover, C60-Au bound to C-dot-tagged DNA aptamers (curve e) resulted in a highly amplified signal. Thus, effective electron transfer promoted effective energy transfer, leading to efficient activation of C-dots. This could be explained by the convenient transmission provided by C60-Au [40,41]. C60 accepted a small number of electrons from Au nanoparticles, and C60 became aggressive and superconducting, eventually providing an excellent channel for electron and energy transfer. Therefore, the efficiency of energy transfer was greatly enhanced. In this context, C-dots may also receive more energy, and the ECL intensity may be amplified. After the target molecule was captured, the ECL intensity of C-dots was obviously reduced (curve f) owing to binding of the aptamers to CBF and the subsequent structural change; these effects attenuated the transfer of energy from C60-Au to C-dots and showed that an effective ECRET occurred between C60-Au and C-dots. The mechanism of ECL generation was illustrated by the following schemes (1–5) [42]:
(1)
CDs −
e −
→
CDs
· +
(2)
Au −
e −
→
Au
· +
(3)
C 60 +
e −
→
C 60
· −
(4)
CDs
· +
+
C 60
· −
→
CDs
∗
+ C 60
(5)
CDs
∗
→ CDs + h υ

Under a certain potential, C-dots (indicated as CDs in the schemes) and Au were motivated and radical cation of C-dots·+ and Au·+ were generated, while C60 accepted electrons and became radical anions, which reacted with and excited C-dots·+ (CDs∗). When these excited CDs∗ returned to the ground state, ECL was generated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimising the response of the aptasensor to CBF
ce:section.ce:para: If the potential was small (less than 0.3 V), it would be difficult to generate ECL. Conversely, if the potential was too high (higher than 0.8 V), the ECL peaks became broader. More than that, tailing of peaks occurred. Therefore, an appropriate CV range is critical for generation of ECL. Research has shown that a cycle of −0.3 V to +0.8 V provides optimal potential. Additionally, ECRET exhibited improved efficiency in 0.05 mol L−1 Tris-HCl compared with that in 0.05 mol L−1 phosphate buffer, citric acid buffer, borate buffer, or acetate buffer. This effect may be explained by the slight alkalinity of Tris-HCl, which would promote the deprotonation of C60 [43]. Thus, more C60·- was obtained, which reacted with C-dots·+ and produced more excited CDs∗, eventually leading to generation of a stronger ECL. Moreover, analysis of the optimal pH demonstrated that the efficiency of ECRET continued to increase as the pH increased, with the highest productivity observed at pH 7.8, as shown in Fig. 5 . When the pH value was above 7.8, the efficiency was decreased. Therefore, we selected 0.05 mol L−1 Tris-HCl (pH 7.8) as the optimal buffer (curve a). CBF adsorption time also affected ECL intensity; the CBF could be completely adsorbed after 10 min (Fig. 5, curve b).

Under optimised conditions, the ECL intensity was measured after CBF adsorption on the sensor at different concentrations. The experimental results showed that there was a relationship between the CBF concentration c and the increase in the current intensity ΔI in the range of 2.0 × 10−11 mol L−1 to 8.0 × 10−9 mol L−1. As shown in Fig. 6 , the linear regression fit was ΔI = 1.55 c (10−11 mol L−1) + 22.97, with a correlation coefficient r of 0.9993. The detection limit (DL) was 8.8 × 10−13 mol L−1 (DL = KSb/a, K = 3). Compared with other methods in the literature, the aptasensor sensor with ECRET was more sensitive and had a lower detection limit. The results are shown in Table 1 .

Next, the selectivity of the aptasensor was examined. The detection procedure was performed as described above using other commercial pesticides, including 1 × 10−7 mol L−1 aldicarb, imidacloprid, abamectin, dimethomorph, acetamiprid, carbary, metolcarb, pirimicarb, isoprocarb, fenobucarb, and tsumacide, in 2.0 × 10−9 mol L−1 CBF. As shown in Fig. 7 , a stronger signal was obtained when the aptasensor did not adsorb CBF. However, the ECL intensity was significantly reduced in the presence of CBF. Importantly, ECL tests showed that there were minor changes (the relative deviation was less than 4.0%) with or without addition of the above-mentioned pesticides. Thus, these pesticides, having structured similar to that of CBF, were not identified by aptamers. Interference by coexisting ions on the detection of CBF was also tested. The results showed that the ECL intensity was slightly altered (the relative deviation was less than 5.0%) in solutions with or without a mixture of 1000-fold (relative to the concentration of CBF) CO3 2−, SO3 2−, Na+, K+, NO3 −, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, Cl−, F−, NH4 + and I−; and 500-fold Fe3+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Br−, Ba2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Pd2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and Mn2+. These results strongly suggested that the aptamer sensor exhibited good selectivity for CBF.

The reproducibility and stability of the sensor were also tested in 2 × 10−9 mol L−1 CBF solution. A relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.23% was obtained by measurement of the ECL intensities using five different sensors fabricated under the same conditions mentioned, indicating good sensor-to-sensor reproducibility. Additionally, an RSD of 0.23% was obtained by measurement of the ECL intensities using the same sensor in five measurements. The intra- and inter-assay RSDs were less than 5.0%, indicating that the sensor had good reproducibility. The sensors were stored at −4 °C when not in use. The same concentration of CBF was detected after 1, 2, 5, 7, and 10 days, and the ECL intensities were nearly unchanged. Therefore, we showed that the sensor exhibited good stability.

The proposed method was applied to the detection of CBF in real fruit and vegetable samples. The sample pretreatment procedure outlined in Section 2.5 was performed. The detection results of our sensor were compared with the results of HPLC-MS analysis used the same samples. As shown in Table 2 , we obtained RSDs of less than 5.0% and recoveries in the range of 90.3–112.0%. And the detection results of our sensor were consistent with those of an HPLC-MS method. These analyses demonstrated that the developed sensor could be used to analyse the amount of CBF in real samples.

In summary, we established a methodology for sensitive, selective, convenient, and quantitative CBF detection using ECL energy transfer and an aptamer biosensor. Effective energy transfer was observed between the C60-Au composite and C-dot-tagged DNA aptamer, resulting in strong signal amplification. DNA aptamers could realise rapid capture of the target molecule, affecting signal detection and ultimately achieving quantitative detection of the target molecule. This sensor could be using for detection of pesticides and was low cost, without the requirement for large, expensive equipment. Importantly, this strategy could be easily extended to the detection of other pesticide residues and harmful substances by screening for proper DNA aptamer sequences.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support received from the Fundamental Scientific Research Funds for Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences (grant nos. 1630042015011 and 1630042015009).
